segments.title: Inspecting the Queen Elizabeth Maximus
segments.summary: The opening inspection establishes a British maximum design with 16.5-inch main guns and rapid-firing 4.5-inch secondaries.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins by inspecting the Queen Elizabeth Maximus carried over from an earlier design session. The ship mounts 16.5-inch main guns whose barrel length he wants to keep within a plausible 42- to 45-calibre range. Its triple 4.5-inch secondary mountings provide a substantial rate of fire. The opening establishes the British reference ship before he builds a German counterpart under comparable historical constraints.

evidence.note: The transcript inspects the Queen Elizabeth Maximus and discusses the calibre length of its 16.5-inch guns and its triple 4.5-inch secondaries.

segments.title: Building Germany's maximum 1912 counterpart
segments.summary: The opposing German design is rebuilt around plausible guns, armour, speed, and the physical limits imposed by the Kiel Canal.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke rejects the game's mixed battery and rebuilds the German counterpart as a heavily protected capital ship with twelve 15-inch guns. Triple turrets require an explicit counterfactual in which Germany obtains the mounting from Skoda, while the hull remains narrow and relatively slow because it must pass through the Kiel Canal. He also checks torpedo calibre, fire control, machinery, armour, and crew quality rather than treating displacement as the only constraint. The finished design is meant to approximate the largest credible German answer to the British Queen Elizabeth Maximus, not an unrestricted game optimum.

evidence.note: The stream rebuilds the German ship around 15-inch guns, Skoda triple turrets, protection, fire control, speed, and Kiel Canal constraints.

segments.title: Restoring a plausible German torpedo and gun battery
segments.summary: The autogenerated opponent is stripped of deck torpedoes and an anachronistic mixed battery in favor of German underwater tubes and a rational dreadnought armament.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke first checks whether the game's German opponent resembles a plausible 1912 capital ship. He removes deck-mounted tubes but retains underwater torpedo tubes, noting that Germany fielded 24-inch weapons during the First World War even though the 1920 game setup offers only smaller ones. He also rejects the 8.5-inch intermediate battery, because combining it with 6.1- and 3.5-inch weapons turns the ship back toward a pre-dreadnought arrangement. The revision shows that historical reconstruction requires correcting equipment categories, not merely matching displacement and headline gun calibre.

evidence.note: The transcript replaces deck tubes with underwater torpedo tubes, notes German 24-inch weapons, and removes an intermediate battery that made the design resemble a pre-dreadnought.

segments.title: Why is the German design heavier to port?
segments.summary: A live design check traces an unexplained port-side weight offset to one extra gun and restores the ship's symmetry.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke pauses when the design reports more weight to port than to starboard. He asks viewers to inspect the fittings, and the live exchange identifies an extra gun on one side. Correcting the asymmetric mount restores the balance readout. The moment demonstrates why individual secondary fittings and their exact placement matter to a capital ship's weight distribution.
segments.question: Why is the German design heavier to port than to starboard?
segments.answerShort: An extra gun had been placed on the port side; identifying and correcting it restored the design's balance.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke asks viewers to find the port-side weight offset, an extra gun is identified, and the layout is balanced.

segments.title: Fire control and the Kiel Canal shape the German maximum
segments.summary: Advanced German rangefinding is retained, but a narrow canal-limited hull and roughly 24-knot target constrain the design.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke does not assume that British size and speed automatically confer every technical advantage. He retains the German ship's enhanced electrical fire control and coincidence rangefinder because they offer credible gains in aiming speed and accuracy. He then narrows the hull and manages draft so the ship can pass through the Kiel Canal, accepting that this works against the attempt to push speed upward. The resulting roughly 24-knot target turns naval infrastructure into a hard design limit alongside armour, armament, and available tonnage.

evidence.note: The transcript retains German fire-control advantages, then narrows the hull for the Kiel Canal and settles near a 24-knot target.

segments.title: Scaling speed and armour for a maximum Queen Elizabeth
segments.summary: The British design balances a 28- to 29-knot target with battleship-grade protection scaled for 16.5-inch guns.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the ship as an upper-bound version of what Britain might have produced in 1912 rather than simply maximizing game statistics. A 28-knot target reflects the ambition behind the historical Queen Elizabeth class, while the game permits the design to reach about 29 knots without abandoning battleship protection. He scales the class's 15-inch guns and 13-inch belt to a roughly 14.5-inch belt for the larger proposed weapons. The final half-inch also reflects the practical increments in which armour plate would be manufactured.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the intended speed and the armour ratio and plate increments used for the British design.

segments.title: Why British and German capital ships diverged
segments.summary: British global commitments and German North Sea priorities produce different trade-offs in speed, armour, and naval spending.
segments.body: The comparison is not framed as a simple contest of technical competence. Dr. Clarke argues that Germany could not freely match Britain's maximum designs because the Kaiserliche Marine competed with the army for funding, while Britain had to build ships able to operate worldwide. In his shorthand, that encourages Germany to value protection while Britain accepts the demands of speed and global reach. The resulting design differences therefore reflect budgets, service priorities, and strategic geography as much as engineering skill.

evidence.note: The stream contrasts German armour priorities and funding limits with Britain's worldwide naval role.

segments.title: A turret explosion turns the first trial into a survival test
segments.summary: The first battle shows how a lost turret, depleted ammunition, flooding, and exposed casemates can overturn an otherwise tough battleship design.
segments.body: A main turret explodes early, taking a large share of the British ship's heavy ammunition with it. The damaged vessel remains afloat and continues fighting with secondary guns and torpedoes, but progressive flooding leaves the crew facing conditions in which abandoning ship would be more realistic than continuing the action. Dr. Clarke notes that a severe list also interferes with gun depression, unless a ship deliberately adopts an exceptional expedient such as USS Texas flooding one side to gain elevation. The trial therefore tests magazine arrangements, ammunition endurance, watertight survival, and weapon placement as much as nominal armour thickness.

evidence.note: The battle loses a turret and much of its ammunition, then continues through flooding, list, secondary fire, and attempted torpedo use.

segments.title: Redesigning protection and secondaries after the failed trial
segments.summary: The failed battle drives a redesign of barbette protection, secondary armament, anti-aircraft guns, speed, and the German opponent's survivability.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke responds to the turret and ammunition failure by revisiting flash-fire protection and the main ship's secondary battery. He tests 5.5-inch and six-inch guns, adds three-inch anti-aircraft weapons, and accepts a lower 28-knot speed to keep the revised arrangement within weight. On the German ship he checks fire control, propulsion, armour, range, and bulkheads, producing what he calls a floating lump of steel. Together, the changes show iterative warship design as a response to observed failure rather than a one-time search for maximum statistics.

evidence.note: The post-battle redesign revises barbette protection, secondaries, anti-aircraft guns, speed, German machinery, armour, and bulkheads.

segments.title: Six-inch secondaries and three-inch anti-aircraft guns
segments.summary: The revised British ship uses six-inch secondaries against torpedo threats and adds the three-inch anti-aircraft weapons common to the period.
segments.body: The redesign uses secondary weapons to answer threats that the main battery is poorly suited to handle. Dr. Clarke favors six-inch turrets because British battleships were expected to counter torpedo-carrying attackers rather than devote every ton to heavy guns. He then adds three-inch anti-aircraft weapons, which he describes as a common British fit of the period. The resulting arrangement connects secondary calibre and placement to doctrine: protection against smaller attackers and aircraft is part of a battleship's combat system, not decorative surplus.

evidence.note: The transcript selects six-inch weapons for anti-torpedo work and adds the three-inch anti-aircraft guns associated with British practice.

segments.title: When historical battleship designs meet game AI
segments.summary: The stream uses UAD as a design experiment while separating historical plausibility from campaign-optimal choices.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that a historically plausible design can perform poorly against the game's AI, whose preferred choices do not always mirror historical doctrine. That tension is central to the stream: the design exercise asks what contemporary industry and doctrine might support, while the battle engine rewards other compromises. The distinction matters whenever a simulated result is used to think through a historical counterfactual. He therefore treats UAD as a controlled design aid whose outputs require historical interpretation, not as an automatic verdict on a real warship.

evidence.note: He distinguishes historical design testing from campaign play optimized for the game.

segments.title: Georgios Averof and the speed of a mixed fleet
segments.summary: A background image prompts a brief lesson on how Georgios Averof's speed let the Greek flagship leave its slower fleet and operate independently against the Ottomans.
segments.body: An image of a Greek-Ottoman naval action prompts Dr. Clarke to single out Georgios Averof as the decisive Greek ship. He describes the flagship as operating so effectively that it went around taking out the opposition. In a later callback, he explains that its commander at one point left the rest of the Greek fleet behind because the other ships could not keep up. The aside shows how a fast flagship could be released from a slower formation when fleet speed otherwise constrained it.

evidence.note: A background image prompts the comment that Georgios Averof dominated the Greek-Ottoman naval action.
The later callback says the Greek flagship left the slower rest of the fleet behind and operated on its own.

segments.title: Why Skoda triple turrets strain the German counterfactual
segments.summary: A twelve-gun German ship requires access to Skoda triple turrets that institutional pride made unlikely to be adopted historically.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that his German design depends on purchasing triple-turret expertise from Skoda. He argues that German observers examined Austro-Hungarian mountings but discounted them because they assumed an Austrian product could not be as good as claimed. That attitude matters because the Tegetthoff-class arrangement offered a practical path to a heavier broadside. The counterfactual therefore depends not just on engineering availability, but on whether a naval institution is willing to trust and procure a foreign ally's design.

evidence.note: The transcript says the German triple turret would have to come from Skoda and discusses German dismissal of Austro-Hungarian designs.

segments.title: The trade-off between 16.5-inch and 18-inch guns
segments.summary: A 16.5-inch battery may be more attractive than an 18-inch battery when rate of fire and firepower are weighed together.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke suggests that a capable 16.5-inch gun might have removed much of the case for pursuing an 18-inch weapon. In this comparison, the larger weapon brings a slower rate of fire, while the smaller heavy gun could deliver more sustained firepower. The useful measure is therefore the weight and frequency of fire that can be maintained, not simply the diameter of one shell. It is a useful reminder that calibre alone does not settle the value of a main battery.

evidence.note: The transcript compares 16.5-inch and 18-inch guns through rate of fire and firepower.

segments.title: Small-calibre hits expose the fire-control system
segments.summary: Repeated 3.4-inch hits damage fire control and prompt a wider redesign of towers, beam, and secondary armament.
segments.body: The German ship's rapid 3.4-inch fire is not penetrating the main belt, but it repeatedly damages the British fire-control system. Dr. Clarke traces the vulnerability to the low-level tower arrangement and rebuilds the ship with a more advanced main tower and a compact secondary tower. Fitting the new structures requires increasing beam and reconsidering the placement of the extra main turret. He then adds six-inch and three-inch weapons because the simulated opponent persistently forces the action into secondary range.

evidence.note: The stream identifies 3.4-inch hits as the source of fire-control damage and redesigns towers, beam, and secondaries in response.

segments.title: HMS Furious exposes the game's 18-inch-gun gap
segments.summary: HMS Furious proves that Britain's 18-inch gun already existed even though UAD's 1920 technology tree withholds it from the design test.
segments.body: When the 1920 technology tree refuses to provide an 18-inch weapon, Dr. Clarke points to HMS Furious as proof that the Royal Navy already possessed such a gun. The obstacle is therefore the software's chronology rather than a physical impossibility in the historical setting. He later moves the design exercise to a newer in-game year so the weapon becomes available without altering the stock rules. The example separates historical design judgment from the representational limits of the simulation.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke cites HMS Furious as evidence that 18-inch guns existed despite their absence from the game's 1920 technology.
He explains that he will change the in-game year rather than add mods when the standardized setup lacks the required weapon.

segments.title: Could the design use larger torpedoes?
segments.summary: A chat suggestion about larger torpedoes reveals a gap between the game's 1920 equipment and historical British and German weapons.
segments.body: The game allows only 20-inch torpedoes in this design year, so Dr. Clarke cannot enlarge them further within the available rules. He points out that Germany had used 24-inch torpedoes during the First World War and Britain was introducing weapons of that size on the Nelson class in the 1920s. The answer distinguishes a limitation of the software's technology tree from the historical equipment that a counterfactual designer could reasonably consider.
segments.question: Could the design use larger torpedoes?
segments.answerShort: Not within this 1920 game setup: it caps the design at 20 inches, even though British and German navies historically used 24-inch torpedoes.

evidence.note: A suggestion of bigger torpedoes is answered with the game's 20-inch limit and historical examples of 24-inch weapons.

segments.title: Why not use mods for the design tests?
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke avoids mods so the design experiment remains standardized and repeatable for viewers using the same game version.
segments.body: Mods could provide missing equipment, but they would also make the result dependent on a particular modification, generation, and interaction with the base game. Dr. Clarke therefore keeps these design tests standardized and replicable. When the stock technology cannot represent an 18-inch gun in the selected year, his practical workaround is to move the scenario to a later date rather than silently changing the ruleset.
segments.question: Are there mods that could add the missing technology?
segments.answerShort: Possibly, but Dr. Clarke avoids them here because the test is meant to be standardized and repeatable with the same game setup.

evidence.note: He answers a question about mods by explaining the need for standardized, replicable design tests.

segments.title: Why six-inch secondaries transform the UAD fight
segments.summary: Adding a strong six-inch secondary battery solves a game problem, but Dr. Clarke cautions that it reflects UAD's close-range AI more than real dreadnought tactics.
segments.body: The revised design exchanges some long-range main-battery power for a much heavier six-inch battery and becomes markedly more effective in the game. Dr. Clarke attributes this to UAD opponents repeatedly closing to secondary range. He contrasts that behavior with real battleships, which would normally seek to keep destroyers and their torpedoes at distance rather than charge into a secondary-gun fight. He also uses the result to underline the Royal Navy's long association with the six-inch gun, while warning that the successful simulated fit answers the game's behavior rather than proving an optimal historical design.

evidence.note: The stream links the heavier six-inch battery to UAD engagement behavior and real-world doctrine.

segments.title: Scaling armour for an 18-inch Agincourt
segments.summary: An imagined modernized Agincourt receives 18-inch guns and a protection scheme derived from British Queen Elizabeth-era ratios.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke builds the proposed ship around 18-inch, 42-calibre guns, then returns to the Queen Elizabeth class to estimate protection. Using the earlier gun-to-armour ratio as a guide, he places the proposed main belt at about 16 inches and adjusts decks, turrets, barbettes, and other protected areas around that choice. He treats the hull as having undergone a far-reaching interwar modernization, including an all-or-nothing protection scheme and updated machinery. The result is not offered as a documented design but as a historically constrained thought experiment inside the game.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the 18-inch gun choice and the resulting armour calculations.

segments.title: Would a refitted Agincourt retain torpedoes?
segments.summary: A chat question prompts a decision on whether the modernized 18-inch ship would retain its torpedo armament.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the refit as extensive enough that the original torpedo installation would probably not survive. His choice is to remove the torpedoes and devote the revised arrangement to the ship's secondary battery instead. The exchange shows how a hypothetical refit can force a choice between inherited fittings and a new combat role.
segments.question: Would the modernized 18-inch Agincourt still carry torpedoes after its refit?
segments.answerShort: Probably not: the refit is extensive enough that Dr. Clarke expects the torpedoes to be removed in favor of the revised secondary arrangement.

evidence.note: He asks chat whether torpedoes would remain, then decides on no torpedoes.

segments.title: Giving Agincourt a Warspite-style modernization
segments.summary: After removing torpedoes, the hypothetical refit adopts a Warspite-style 4.5-inch secondary battery and redistributes protection and weight.
segments.body: Once the torpedo decision is made, Dr. Clarke treats the ship as receiving a deep Queen Elizabeth-style modernization rather than a light update. The new secondary fit uses 4.5-inch, 45-calibre guns in a layout compared with Warspite's reconstruction. He also adjusts deck armour beneath the main battery and watches fore-and-aft weight balance as the fittings move. The passage makes the counterfactual coherent: an old hull carrying 18-inch weapons must be rebuilt as a complete interwar combat system, not simply rearmed.

evidence.note: The stream gives the ship a Warspite-style refit with 4.5-inch guns, more deck protection, and corrected fore-and-aft balance.

segments.title: German limits in an 18-inch-gun world
segments.summary: The opposing Bismarck-like design is constrained by German infrastructure, industrial capacity, and historically plausible armament choices.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke rejects several game-generated features because they do not fit the industrial or design context he is trying to model. He argues that Germany could not physically build an equivalent giant ship with the same infrastructure, then reshapes the opposing vessel around more plausible 15-inch and 5.9-inch armament, range, and protection choices. He checks the historical ship's speed trials, cruising range, Kiel Canal dimensions, radar and stereoscopic fire control, machinery, secondary battery, and anti-aircraft fit. The resulting ten-gun vessel represents an enhanced Bismarck that Germany might attempt in this altered arms race, rather than an unrestricted fantasy design. Its limitations remain industrial and infrastructural even after the counterfactual gives German designers a more dangerous British opponent.

evidence.note: The stream discusses German infrastructure, realistic armament, range, and the Bismarck-like opponent.

segments.title: Testing Bismarck's speed, range, and canal limits
segments.summary: Trial speed, realistic cruising range, and Kiel Canal dimensions keep the enhanced Bismarck within German operating constraints.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke checks the counterfactual opponent against Bismarck's practical performance rather than accepting the game's maximum settings. He retains the roughly 30-knot trial speed but rejects a range calculation that implies 20,000 kilometres at full speed, contrasting it with about 16,430 kilometres at 19 knots. Draft and beam are then adjusted around passage through the Kiel Canal. The completed ten-gun ship is therefore an enhanced Bismarck within the scenario's altered arms race, while German infrastructure and endurance still limit what the design can do.

evidence.note: The transcript checks Bismarck's trial speed and historical range, adjusts draft and beam for the Kiel Canal, and completes the enhanced design.

segments.title: The narrow range window favoring the Bismarck-like ship
segments.summary: The Bismarck-like opponent has a small range band in which its 15-inch guns can engage before Agincourt's 18-inch battery can answer.
segments.body: Before launching the battle, Dr. Clarke compares the armour-piercing ranges of the two ships. The German design reaches about 32.5 kilometres while the British 18-inch design initially reaches about 30.6, leaving roughly a kilometre and a half in which the German ship can fire unanswered. That advantage exists only if the faster Bismarck-like ship can maintain the correct separation. The comparison turns engagement geometry and fire-control choices into operational variables rather than treating gun calibre as an automatic victory condition.

evidence.note: The pre-battle comparison gives the German ship about 32.5 kilometres of armour-piercing range against roughly 30.6 for Agincourt.

segments.title: Why one battle doctrine cannot fit every naval era
segments.summary: A close-range attack that suits pre-dreadnought combat becomes self-defeating when the same AI applies it to long-range dreadnoughts.
segments.body: The Bismarck-like ship repeatedly closes toward secondary range even though its speed and gun range should favor keeping distance. Dr. Clarke suggests that the game's common AI doctrine may work better for pre-dreadnoughts, whose mixed batteries reward closing, than for dreadnoughts built around long-range heavy guns. He contrasts this with an earlier version whose behavior seemed better suited to dreadnought combat. The result illustrates why command logic must change with weapons, ranges, and ship type instead of applying one tactical rule across naval eras.

evidence.note: The battle closes to secondary range and the commentary contrasts tactics suitable for pre-dreadnoughts with those needed by dreadnoughts.

segments.title: Trading shell weight for interwar gun range
segments.summary: Lighter shells extend the hypothetical 18-inch battery's range while protection remains heavy, echoing Nelson and Rodney's long-range priorities.
segments.body: To recover range, Dr. Clarke selects a lighter shell and brings the 18-inch battery to about 32.3 kilometres. He argues that this is historically plausible because Britain accepted lighter projectiles on Nelson and Rodney, and an interwar capital ship would seek the longest useful engagement range it could obtain. The choice does not turn the vessel into a lightly protected sniper: the design still carries a 16-inch belt and turret faces with very heavy barbettes. Together, shell weight, gun range, and armour are traded as one design problem rather than treating calibre as the only meaningful variable.

evidence.note: The stream selects lighter shells for extra range, invokes Nelson and Rodney, and retains the ship's heavy belt, turret, and barbette protection.

segments.title: Could players choose the AI's attack doctrine?
segments.summary: A viewer proposes selectable AI attack plans so each ship can use tactics appropriate to its weapons and era.
segments.body: A viewer suggests letting the player assign an attack strategy to an AI-controlled captain before the fight. Dr. Clarke agrees because one standardized behavior cannot exploit every design's range, speed, and battery. Manual control remains possible, but managing the ship while observing the enemy and the wider battle creates a substantial command workload. Selectable doctrine would let the simulation distinguish tactical intent without requiring the player to micromanage every movement.
segments.question: Would it help if players could choose the AI captain's attack strategy?
segments.answerShort: Yes. Dr. Clarke says selectable strategy would be useful because manually coordinating the ship, watching the enemy, and managing the action is difficult to do all at once.

evidence.note: A viewer suggests selectable AI attack strategies and Dr. Clarke agrees, citing the workload of coordinating and observing the action manually.

segments.title: An 18-inch Agincourt against a Bismarck-like opponent
segments.summary: The matchup illustrates the destructive potential of an 18-inch battery, while emphasizing the need to maintain range.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke imagines an upgraded Agincourt carrying eight 18-inch guns against a Bismarck-like ship and argues that the latter would have strong incentives not to accept the fight. The faster German vessel's realistic choice would be to disengage rather than test its protection against the much larger shells. The massive British weapons are presented as decisive once they land, especially at closer ranges. At the same time, the game exposes a weakness: allowing such a ship into a close, secondary-gun engagement invites catastrophic magazine risk.

evidence.note: The battle discussion covers the 18-inch ship's power, range, and close-range vulnerability.

segments.title: How an 18-inch prewar flagship could reshape the naval race
segments.summary: Even a single Royal Navy ship with eight 18-inch guns before the First World War would force other navies to reconsider their own capital-ship plans.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke moves from the duel to the international consequences of commissioning such a ship before the First World War. Britain might describe the vessel as a one-off fleet flagship, but other powers would still have to plan around an eight-gun, 18-inch battery. Its significance would therefore extend beyond the ship's own combat record into budgets, infrastructure, gun development, and counter-designs. The moment is a useful reminder that naval arms races respond to perceived capability as well as to the number of hulls actually built.

evidence.note: The transcript asks what other navies would build if an eight-gun, 18-inch Royal Navy flagship entered service before the First World War.

segments.title: Reconstructing Yamato for the comparison
segments.summary: Yamato is recreated as a modern battleship with historically informed speed, 18.1-inch guns, armour, and secondary armament.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke resists treating Yamato as an abstract super-battleship and instead works from its historical characteristics. He classifies it as a modern battleship, then sets the main armament to nine 18-inch, 46-calibre guns and the design speed to roughly 27 knots. He reconstructs the heavy belt, deck and turret protection, cruising range, machinery, secondary battery, and smaller anti-aircraft weapons as closely as the game permits. The resulting ship is intentionally overweight compared with the historical vessel because the available components cannot reproduce every detail. It nevertheless gives the hypothetical Agincourt a recognizable late-war opponent rather than an arbitrary game hull.

evidence.note: The stream reconstructs Yamato's main battery, speed, armour, and secondary battery.

segments.title: Four twin turrets or three triples?
segments.summary: The Agincourt–Yamato test compares four twin 18-inch turrets with three triples while acknowledging major differences in mass, speed, and range.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke identifies the encounter as a useful way to think about turret arrangement. The two designs have broadly comparable heavy guns, but one carries eight guns in four twin mountings while the other has nine guns in three triple mountings. Agincourt has a slight range and speed advantage, while the reconstructed Yamato outweighs it by about 35,000 tons, roughly the displacement of a treaty battleship. The game also cannot reproduce the historical dispersion problems associated with Yamato's real mountings. The trade-off between an additional turret and an additional gun is therefore visible, but the surrounding ships are not controlled laboratory equivalents.

evidence.note: The comparison covers gun and turret counts, a slight Agincourt range and speed advantage, and Yamato's roughly 35,000-ton mass advantage.

segments.title: Range advantage cannot remove magazine risk
segments.summary: The final trials show why a long-range advantage still depends on avoiding close action and surviving magazine hits.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke repeatedly instructs the simulated ship not to close, since its range advantage should let it fight at distance. The game's distant view makes the opposing battleship appear as a small speck, which he praises as a more realistic impression of long-range naval combat. Even after he adds deck protection and retains reinforced bulkheads, however, the outcome again turns on a magazine hit. He closes by returning to the counterfactual: a six-gun, 18-inch Agincourt modernized for the Second World War would be a dangerous predator. Its immense shells and magazines also make the consequences of a successful hit severe, so range and protection reduce risk without removing it.

evidence.note: The closing trials emphasize keeping range, magazine detonations, and the final Agincourt counterfactual.
